education.notes: — Yale 1808. Son of Sarah Edwards (H00936) and Benjamin Chaplin Jr. (Yale 1778). Born posthumously one month after his father died. Methodist minister, educator, Whig gubernatorial candidate Michigan 1845. His grandfather Benjamin Chaplin (Yale 1778) also in Yale connections. (Yale Biographical Sketches, Dexter 1912)

cross_connections.display_label: is the mother of Mary Floyd Cushman, who married Edwards Williams

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of the Mendocino Lumber Company

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Mendocino Lumber Company
narrative_blocks.body: Williams surveyed Big River for its redwoods in 1852 and crossed Panama to buy milling machinery shipped around Cape Horn. He founded and long led the Mendocino Lumber Company, the redwood mill that built the coastal town.

narrative_blocks.header: A New York Volunteer in the Mexican War
narrative_blocks.body: Williams served with the 1st New York Volunteers in the Mexican-American War and reached California in 1847, on the eve of the Gold Rush. He lived to witness the 1906 San Francisco earthquake from his lumber company's ruined offices.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a distant Hooker cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Williams and his wife Mary Floyd Cushman both descended from the Reverend Thomas Hooker through separate colonial lines, he through the Edwards family and she through the Tallmadges. Their daughters thus carried a double descent from Hartford's founder.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The Big River redwood mill he founded, now in Mendocino Headlands State Park.

bio.bio_blurb: Founder of the Mendocino Lumber Company

narrative_blocks.header: Josiah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah died in 1824, aged about three.

narrative_blocks.header: Sarah died young
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died in 1826, aged about two.

notable.notable_blurb: Union artillery general who commanded the Reserve at Gettysburg

military_service.unit: Artillery Reserve, Army of the Potomac
military_service.notes: Commanded the Artillery Reserve at Gettysburg; severely wounded at Cold Harbor; deputy quartermaster general after the war.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Commanded the Army of the Potomac's Artillery Reserve, honored by this Gettysburg monument.

cross_connections.display_label: was his uncle and also a Union brigadier general

narrative_blocks.header: His guns helped break Pickett's Charge
narrative_blocks.body: At Gettysburg he commanded the Army of the Potomac's Artillery Reserve, whose massed guns helped repel the Confederate assault on the third day. Two horses were shot from under him during the fighting.

narrative_blocks.header: Nephew of the general who founded Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Daniel Tyler also wore a Union general's star and later founded the Alabama city of Anniston. Both men descended from the theologian Jonathan Edwards through their Connecticut family.

narrative_blocks.header: Shot at Cold Harbor, he never rode again
narrative_blocks.body: A bullet shattered his ankle at Cold Harbor in 1864, ending his field command. He was brevetted major general for gallantry and served out the war at a desk.

narrative_blocks.header: He finished his memoir, then died at forty-two
narrative_blocks.body: His war injuries wore at his health, and he died young. He had just completed an autobiography, published in 1878 and still in print.

narrative_blocks.header: He soldiered the frontier before the war
narrative_blocks.body: Before the Civil War he campaigned in the Yakima War in the Pacific Northwest and the Utah War, crossing the young frontier as an artillery officer. Gettysburg lay years ahead.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran a Southern railroad from Philadelphia
narrative_blocks.body: George Frederick Tyler became the first president of the Norfolk and Western Railroad. He spent his career and fortune in Philadelphia, far from the family's Connecticut roots.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at thirty-one, her widower died in London
narrative_blocks.body: She died at thirty-one in Philadelphia, far from the Hartford family she was born into. Her husband Cyprian Beach outlived her by twenty-seven years and died in London.

narrative_blocks.header: A later brother was given his name
narrative_blocks.body: The first Edwin Tyler died at two. The family later gave the name to a son born in 1834.

narrative_blocks.header: She died before her fourth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Grey Tyler, a daughter of Frederick and Sophia Tyler, died at three.

orbit_connections.name: Theodore Roosevelt (X00744) — married great-granddaughter of Daniel Tyler's parents
orbit_connections.relationship: Through his parents Sarah Edwards Tyler (H00936) and Capt. Daniel Tyler, Brig. Gen. Tyler is an ancestor of Edith Kermit Carow (1861-1948), who became Theodore Roosevelt's second wife and First Lady.

notable.notable_blurb: Union Civil War general and ironmaster

cross_connections.display_label: built an iron empire in Gilded Age Los Angeles
was his nephew and rose to Union general too

bio.bio_blurb: Union general, ironmaster, founder of Anniston

military_service.unit: 1st Connecticut Infantry; division, Army of Northeastern Virginia
military_service.notes: Commanded a division at First Bull Run; led a brigade at the Siege of Corinth; briefly commanded Camp Douglas.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Tyler commanded a division at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 and absorbed much of the blame for the Union defeat, mustered out within weeks. Recommissioned in 1862, he led troops at the siege of Corinth and briefly ran the Camp Douglas prison camp.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Alabama city of Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning from the army in 1864, Tyler built blast furnaces and ran railroads, finding far more success in rails than in pig iron. In the 1870s he established the Woodstock Iron Company and helped found Anniston, named for his daughter-in-law Annie.

narrative_blocks.header: West Point artillerist who studied in France
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from West Point in 1819, Tyler became an authority on artillery, studying at the French school at Metz and translating French gunnery manuals into English. His honesty inspecting private arms contractors may have stalled his promotion beyond first lieutenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Anniston was named for his wife Annie
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred ran the Anniston Manufacturing Company in the Alabama iron town his father helped found. The new city took its name from his wife, Annie Scott Tyler, originally 'Annie's Town.'

narrative_blocks.header: From Paris polish to genteel poverty
narrative_blocks.body: Educated in Paris during her father the general’s European years, Gertrude married the shipping heir Charles Carow in 1859 — and watched his drinking dissolve the family fortune. Widowed in 1883, she eventually moved to Italy, where living was cheaper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter grew up with the Roosevelts
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows were intimates of the East 20th Street Roosevelts, and her daughter Edith was raised in and out of Theodore’s nursery — becoming First Lady in 1901, six years after Gertrude died at Turin.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Charles Carow
was the mother of her husband Charles Carow

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: A wedding seven months before death
narrative_blocks.body: At sixty-seven, Major Tyler married Belle Alston Webb in Washington on March 16, 1906 — his only known wife — and died that October in Anniston. His 1904 will had already named their two daughters, Lucy Mason and Nellie Osgood Tyler, by their full maiden names.

narrative_blocks.header: He shared his birthday with Augustus
narrative_blocks.body: Edmund was born May 2, 1838; his youngest brother Augustus arrived May 2, 1851 — the same date, thirteen years apart, in the same Norwich household.

bio.bio_blurb: Major who settled in Tyler-founded Anniston

narrative_blocks.header: She married the Irish captain at thirty-five
narrative_blocks.body: The general’s daughter married Captain Alexander Moore in Manhattan in April 1875; their only child, born two years later in Montgomery, Alabama, became founding commandant of the Army’s School of Fire. She lies beside her husband at Arlington.

narrative_blocks.header: First in French, far from it in drawing
narrative_blocks.body: At West Point he stood first in French and Spanish “without having to work very hard” and was far from distinguished in drawing, his classmate Dorst remembered. Five frontier years with the 4th Cavalry followed — scouting from Fort Clark’s armed camps, where no one walked two hundred yards unarmed.

narrative_blocks.header: Snow-blind on Mackenzie’s winter expedition
narrative_blocks.body: A volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition after the Custer disaster, he rode in Mackenzie’s November 1876 destruction of the Cheyenne village — and left on sick leave, his eyes burned by glare off the snow. He resigned in 1878, “regretted, loved and respected.”

narrative_blocks.header: Seven thousand acres of American tea
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding the 3rd Connecticut Volunteers in South Carolina in 1898, the colonel saw commercial promise in southern tea and bought four Colleton County plantations — Holly Grove, Rutledge Island, Mowbray, Turkey Hill — whose flourishing gardens and factory his son Frederick managed.

narrative_blocks.header: The Russian ambassador took his house
narrative_blocks.body: The stately residence he built at 1634 I Street, facing Farragut Square in Washington, later housed the Russian ambassador. He was president of New London’s Pequot Casino Association at his death, a clubman to the last afternoon.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on his brother’s thirteenth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: May 2, 1851 — thirteen years to the day after his brother Edmund, born May 2, 1838. The Tyler brothers shared a birthday a generation apart.

cross_connections.display_label: was the patent-medicine magnate father of his wife Cornelia
fought at Powder River in March; he rode there in November

bio.bio_blurb: Cavalry officer turned American tea planter

military_service.notes: Texas frontier 1873-78; volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition, Nov-Dec 1876; resigned July 1, 1878
Camps Haven, Meade, and Marion; honorably discharged Jan 31, 1899

career.organization: American Tea Growing Co., Colleton County SC

H00381.prominent_name_reason: Hon. Timothy Edwards (1738-1813) — Princeton 1757; merchant and judge of Stockbridge MA. His son "Edward Hooker Edwards" (b.1763) carried the Hooker name explicitly — a deliberate tribute to the founding ancestor in the 5th generation of Edwards descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Princeton 1757 — son of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Princeton in 1757 and went to Elizabethtown, New Jersey to practice law. He moved to Stockbridge, Massachusetts around 1770, where he served as a judge — earning the 'Hon.' style on his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Ogden family of New Jersey
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Rhoda Ogden was sister of Aaron Ogden (Governor of New Jersey 1812-1813) and Matthias Ogden, a lieutenant colonel who marched to Quebec with Benedict Arnold in 1775. Through her, the Edwards-Hooker line connected to early New Jersey political and military leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Phebe married a Hooker — double convergence
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Phebe Edwards married Rev. Asahel Hooker — both descended from Thomas Hooker, creating a double Hooker convergence in their children. Phebe's path back went through her mother Sarah Pierpont Edwards; Asahel's through the main Hartford Hooker line.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Stoddard — daughter of Rev. Solomon Stoddard of Northampton
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Timothy Edwards of East Windsor CT — minister 60 years until death 1758 age 89

church_roles.notes: Succeeded grandfather Solomon Stoddard. Led Great Awakening revivals. Dismissed 1750 over communion controversy.

X00128.prominent_name_reason: Rev. Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758) — Yale 1720; greatest American theologian; leader of the Great Awakening; delivered "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" (1741); President of Princeton; grandfather of Aaron Burr Jr. (3rd U.S. Vice President). Father's father-in-law was Rev. Timothy Edwards who held one enslaved person named Ansars. Edwards himself enslaved at least six people: Venus (purchased 1731), Joab, Rose Binney, Titus (son of Joab and Rose), Joseph, Sue, and possibly Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Venus
enslaved_persons.description: Purchased June 1731 as a young teenager. Likely later baptized and given name Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Titus
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Son of Joab and Rose.

enslaved_persons.name: Joseph
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Married couple with Sue.

cross_connections.display_label: Giles Hooker Cowles studied theology under him after graduating Yale in 1789.
Succeeded his son-in-law as Princeton president after his sudden death.
Hosted him in final illness; published his Life as bestseller
Physician-minister and intimate friend in his New York years

education.notes: Entered 1716 at age 13. Graduated with highest honors.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1741 sermon shocked New England
narrative_blocks.body: On July 8, 1741, he delivered 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' to a Connecticut congregation. The Great Awakening had been spreading from his Northampton parish for six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his greatest works in frontier exile
narrative_blocks.body: After Northampton he took the Stockbridge mission preaching to Housatonnuck families through an interpreter. There he composed Freedom of the Will, The Nature of True Virtue, and The Doctrine of Original Sin.

narrative_blocks.header: Town turned on him over a midwifery book
narrative_blocks.body: In 1748 he disciplined young people in his Northampton parish for circulating what he called 'immoral books,' later identified as a midwifery manual. His own Williams and Hawley relatives led the council that dismissed him in 1750.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty-five days into the Princeton presidency
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted the College of New Jersey presidency in early 1758 after his son-in-law Aaron Burr Sr. died suddenly in office. A smallpox inoculation administered February 23 turned fatal on March 22.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale valedictorian at sixteen, in Latin
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at twelve and graduated four years later as the youngest member of his class. He delivered the commencement valedictory address in Latin.

sources.short: Life of Rev. Jonathan Edwards by Sereno Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Princeton Cemetery, Princeton, New Jersey — buried next to Jonathan Edwards

cross_connections.display_label: Died trying to bring him home from Princeton at age two.
Died trying to bring her home from Princeton at age four.
Her North Haven nephew — wealthiest man in town in 1786

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Pierpont Edwards (1710-1758), wife of Jonathan Edwards.
bio.bio_blurb: Subject of Edwards’s famous meditation at thirteen

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards wrote about her when she was twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards composed a private spiritual meditation about a 'young lady in New Haven who is beloved of that Great Being who made and rules the world.' She married him five years later, at seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home her orphaned Burr grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: After Jonathan's death she traveled south in autumn 1758 to retrieve four-year-old Sarah Burr and two-year-old Aaron Burr Jr. She fell sick with dysentery in Philadelphia before she could complete the journey.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly six months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan died of smallpox-inoculation complications on March 22, 1758, weeks into the Princeton presidency. Sarah died of dysentery on October 2, 1758, at forty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Made her will the day she died
narrative_blocks.body: On October 2, 1758, in Philadelphia, she dictated her last instructions hours before dysentery took her. The two Burr grandchildren she had been escorting were placed with Dr. William Shippen.

narrative_blocks.header: Her portrait hangs in a Yale dining hall
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband were both painted in oils, traditionally attributed to John Smybert. The portraits were bequeathed to Yale in 1939 and now hang in Jonathan Edwards College.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
